input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes, comprising motors, gearboxes, and controllers, designed for industrial manufacturing applications requiring precise motion control and real-time monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: SysML for Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) structural modeling of system components, interfaces, and connectors, and MathWorks Simulink for dynamic behavior simulation and control algorithm implementation specific to the robot's operational dynamics.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time synchronization with sensor data captured as time series for live-data linking between physical and digital components; specific synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity details are not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Structured according to the Digital Shadow Reference Model (Fig. 1), defining components such as Asset (physical entity representation), Source (data sources), and Processing (data aggregation and abstraction); Concrete Digital Shadow Model (Fig. 5) implements these concepts for specific purposes.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration via MQTT for sensor data streaming, Asset Administration Shells (AAS) as an interoperability standard for semantic data exchange, and connection to Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller for machine control.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) using SysML for multi-level abstraction (requirements, functional, logical, product/technical layers), with Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) like AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs defining component interfaces and interconnections. Behavioral modeling utilizes MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines, supplemented by MathWorks Simulink simulations and code in C++ or Java. Data sources consist of contextual traces from physical objects, aggregated and abstracted per the digital shadow reference model, with integration of heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives across modeling tools.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Live-data linking streams time series sensor data including input current, output angle, and target/actual position values from the physical system to the digital shadow via MQTT protocol.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The digital shadow functions as a passive entity with one-way data flow; it does not push data or commands back to the physical system, as defined in the reference model where communication is strictly physical-to-virtual.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol is used for transmitting time series sensor data between physical and virtual components as the specified communication protocol for network infrastructure.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system utilizes industry standards such as SysML for Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) [17], and architecture description languages including ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26], and SysML IBDs [17], [27] for component & connector-based modeling. These standards facilitate interoperability among heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives [31].
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The physical safety cell of the Mabi robotic arm (Max-100-2-25-p) restricts the maximum working range to [-90°, 90°], ensuring mechanical safety limits during operation.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the system as the 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes' and mentions its components (motors, gearboxes) and domain (industrial manufacturing). However, it lacks specific details present in the text, such as the specific controller (Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl), the encoder setup, and the functional metrics (reach, repeatability). Quotes: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The text describes a 'Digital Shadow' (passive) which includes a 'visualization in a dashboard' and enables friction identification. However, the text explicitly differentiates a Digital Shadow from a 'Digital Twin' (active), stating a DT 'can provide such active components' but does not list specific active services implemented in this work. Thus, specific Digital Twin services are not present.

The extraction accurately identifies SysML for structural modeling and MathWorks Simulink for dynamic behavior simulation. It correctly links them to their specific roles in the system described in the text. Quotes: 'The robot’s structure as a component & connector SysML model', 'Friction within the first axis is modeled with executable simulation models of MathWorks MATLAB Simulink'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', which is correct based on the provided text. The paper focuses on a specific use case: 'the first axis of the robot' and creating 'a digital shadow' for it. The extraction accurately describes the overall orchestration based on the 'Digital Shadow Reference Model (Fig. 1)', defining components such as Asset, Source, and Processing. It correctly identifies the 'Concrete Digital Shadow Model (Fig. 5)' as the implementation of these concepts for the specific use case, supported by the detailed UML diagrams and descriptions in Sections II.B and IV.B.

The extraction correctly identifies integration via 'MQTT' for sensor data streaming, 'Asset Administration Shells (AAS)' for semantic data exchange, and connection to the 'Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl' controller. block_5_judge.reasoning: The document focuses on digital shadows for robot motion control but does not discuss lifecycle phases, transition management, or lifecycle governance approaches as defined in the characteristic description. While it mentions modeling during development phases, there is no comprehensive information about Digital Twin lifecycle stages.

The document outlines a methodology for creating digital shadows (Section II.C) with steps including 'Problem identification,' 'Asset and model analysis,' 'Use or creation of calculations or models,' and 'Data identification.' However, it lacks information about quality assurance practices, testing and validation approaches, version control, and evolution management strategies for Digital Twins as specified in the characteristic description.

While the document mentions that 'The resulting digital shadows enable the continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction' and includes 'visualization in a dashboard,' it lacks detailed information about analytics capabilities, decision making processes, or confidence measures for recommendations as required by the characteristic description.

The description is well-supported by the source text. Section II.A states: 'ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase' and mentions architecture description languages including 'ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML IBDs [17], [27]' for component & connector-based modeling. It also states that 'various heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].'
